title: Layered Background Texture
subtitle: Watercolour wash, tissue couching, washi tape and stamp details in the correct order
excerpt: Building a textured scrapbook background in four layers: a watercolour wash laid first, torn tissue applied while damp for a couching effect, dried flat, then washi tape strips and rubber stamp details added last. Order matters at every stage.
sourceNotes: General scrapbooking practice; no single PD canonical source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique in which thin tissue paper is pressed onto a wet adhesive or wash surface and allowed to dry embedded, creating a slightly raised, translucent texture.
A dilute, even layer of watercolour paint applied across a surface with a loaded brush, producing a flat or gently graded tint.
A scrapbook page base built up from multiple overlapping materials and media, each added in a specific order so earlier layers show through later ones.

content.text: Why order matters
A
layered background
works because each layer is either wet-into-wet (blending together) or dry-over-dry (sitting on top). If you add the
watercolour wash
over dry tape, the tape edge creates a hard line. If you add stamp detail to a damp page, the ink bleeds. The sequence below avoids both problems.
Stage 1: Watercolour wash

Use the page itself as the painting surface, or work on a separate sheet of 130 gsm cartridge paper that will later be glued to the scrapbook page. The second approach prevents the scrapbook from warping.
Mix a dilute watercolour wash in one or two colours. Load a large flat brush or a waterbrush pen.
Wet the paper surface with clean water first, or apply the wash directly if you prefer harder edges at the paper boundary.
Lay even strokes across the full surface. If using two colours, apply the second while the first is still damp and allow them to bleed into each other at the centre of the page.
Leave the paper flat. Do not use a heat tool to speed drying; the wash needs to settle evenly.
Stage 2: Tissue couching while damp
The
tissue couching
stage must happen while the wash is still damp, not wet and not dry. The tissue embeds into the wash surface and, when dry, reads as part of the background rather than something glued on top.

Tear pieces of plain tissue paper (not patterned, not printed) into irregular shapes roughly 3 to 8 cm across. Torn edges are better than cut edges: they feather at the boundary and blend into the wash.
While the wash is still damp, lay the tissue pieces onto the surface. Do not press hard: just place and smooth lightly with a dry fingertip. The damp wash acts as the adhesive.
Overlap pieces if you want a denser texture. Leave gaps if you want the wash to show through in areas.
Optionally brush a very dilute PVA solution (one part PVA to four parts water) over the tissue to secure it. This also increases the sheen slightly.
Leave flat to dry completely. Do not lift the sheet while the tissue is damp or it will wrinkle.
Stage 3: Dry flat and flatten if needed

Once fully dry, the paper may have cockled slightly. Place the sheet face-down on clean scrap paper and press under a flat heavy board and several books for 30 minutes. If there is significant cockling, very lightly mist the reverse side with water and press immediately.
Check the tissue is fully bonded before continuing. Any loose edges should be pressed down with a bone folder and a tiny dot of dilute PVA, then dried again.
Stage 4: Washi tape strips
Apply washi tape strips to the dry background as structural or decorative elements. Tape over tissue areas, at angles, or as borders.

Because washi tape is paper-based, it accepts watercolour and ink applied over it. For a more integrated look, apply the tape first then lightly brush a dilute wash over the tape edges; this softens the hard tape boundary.
Stage 5: Rubber stamp detail
Rubber stamping is the final stage, applied to a fully dry surface. Ink the stamp pad and press firmly and evenly onto the background in the desired positions. Lift cleanly without rocking the stamp.
Over tissue areas, the stamp impression may be slightly uneven. The surface has texture, so the print won't be perfectly even. With floral or geometric stamps this reads as character, not a flaw.

items.symptom: Tissue wrinkles into ridges rather than lying flat
items.cause: Tissue was applied too dry (wash already absorbed into paper) or pressed too hard
items.fix: Dampen the area with a light water spray and re-press the tissue with a damp fingertip. If the wash is too dry to re-wet the tissue, apply dilute PVA mix instead.

items.symptom: Stamp impression smudges or bleeds on the background
items.cause: Background was not fully dry, or washi tape surface has too little texture to hold ink
items.fix: Allow a full 30-minute dry before stamping. Test the stamp on a scrap piece of the same textured background first.

items.symptom: Watercolour wash has tide marks (hard edges where wash pooled)
items.cause: Wet paint left sitting on the paper without being kept moving
items.fix: For future washes, keep the brush moving until the wash is absorbed. For existing tide marks, dampen slightly with clean water and blend the edge; or incorporate them as a feature by emphasising them with a second dilute layer.
